Title: Elucidation of the biosynthetic pathways of timosaponins reveals the antifungal mechanisms in Anemarrhena asphodeloides

Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Highly expressed in rhizomes and lateral roots; low in leaves
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Antifungal defense, especially against Rhizoctonia solani
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: In vitro enzyme assay; antifungal PDA plate assays with Rhizoctonia solani, Fusarium oxysporum, and Alternaria solani at 50 mg/L and dose-response assays
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Converts furostanol-type timosaponins into spirostanol-type timosaponins, increasing formation of the more antifungal timosaponin III-type products
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Recombinant protein expression in E. coli BL21(DE3); in vitro enzyme assay with protodioscin, protogracillin, timosaponin II, and timosaponin N; UPLC-MS product profiling; antifungal assays comparing timosaponin II and III
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. AaF26G1 cleaves the C-26 glucose from multiple furostanol-type steroidal saponins. 2. This reaction generates corresponding spirostanol-type products including timosaponin III from timosaponin II. 3. The conversion is linked to stronger antifungal activity, supporting a plant defense role.
Common_Genes.Omics_Data: Transcriptome data from a published study [23]; accession NA
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Spirostanol products are more bioactive/antifungal and potentially more cytotoxic than stored furostanol forms; no direct plant growth penalty reported
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Acts on multiple substrates with high catalytic activity, indicating broad substrate tolerance among furostanol-type steroidal saponins
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful for engineering enhanced spirostanol-type timosaponin content and antifungal defense; should be deployed with upstream pathway genes that increase furostanol precursor supply

Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Skeleton cyclization step supplying both phytosterol and cholesterol/timosaponin branches
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Supported as key enzyme and rate-limiting step in timosaponin biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Cyclizes 2,3-oxidosqualene to cycloartenol, establishing the sterol/saponin backbone precursor
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Forms the common precursor pool for phytosterols and timosaponins
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Root bark/rhizome-associated pathway; steroid sapogenins accumulate consistent with underground tissues
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Medicinal bioactivity and antifungal defense through downstream timosaponins
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Heterologous expression in yeast p24 and transient tobacco expression; site-directed mutagenesis and molecular docking
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Highest among tested tissues in root bark
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: GAL1 in yeast; pEAQ-HT-DEST1 expression system in tobacco
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Enables cycloartenol production and determines downstream timosaponin biosynthesis capacity
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Yeast heterologous expression; tobacco transient expression; GC-MS; NMR; site-directed mutagenesis; molecular docking
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Among three OSC candidates, only AaOSCR12 produced cycloartenol in yeast and tobacco. 2. It is the only identified CAS in A. asphodeloides and directly determines entry into timosaponin biosynthesis. 3. Six residues (H257, N369, T448, V507, P558, Y616) are essential for catalytic activity.
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Transcriptome data from a published study [23]; accession NA
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Mutations at six critical residues abolish cycloartenol synthesis and would be expected to reduce steroidal saponin accumulation
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Also yielded 31-norcycloartenol in yeast due to endogenous yeast sterol-4α-methyl oxidase complex activity; authors infer natural variation in OSC may underlie geographic variation in saponin content
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Top-priority target for breeding or engineering high-timosaponin cultivars; preserving catalytic residues is critical; suitable for pathway reconstruction and enzyme engineering

Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Supported as a critical slow enzyme controlling Δ5-phytosterol composition; branch-control step
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Transfers a methyl group to C-24 of cycloartenol to form 24-methylenecycloartanol
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Diverts flux away from cholesterol/timosaponin branch toward phytosterols
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Membrane sterol composition and plant sterol biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Expressed in cycloartenol-producing yeast strain R12
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Highly expressed in rhizomes and lateral roots; low in leaves
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Highly expressed in rhizomes and lateral roots
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes phytosterol-branch formation from cycloartenol and competes with SSR2 for precursor flux
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Yeast expression in strain R12; GC-MS metabolite profiling
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. AaSMT1 converted cycloartenol to 24-methylenecycloartanol in yeast. 2. It defines the phytosterol branch competing with cholesterol formation. 3. It also methylated 31-norcycloartenol, indicating substrate promiscuity.
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Transcriptome data from a published study [23]; accession NA
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Increased SMT1 activity may reduce flux to cholesterol-derived timosaponins by competing for cycloartenol
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Also produced cycloeucalenol-derived product from unnatural 31-norcycloartenol in yeast
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Can be tuned to balance phytosterol versus timosaponin production depending breeding goals; caution that stronger SMT1 may lower desired timosaponin accumulation

Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key branch-switch enzyme; not explicitly established as rate-limiting
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Reduces the Δ24(25) side-chain double bond of cycloartenol to form cycloartanol
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Directs precursor flux toward cholesterol and downstream timosaponins
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Medicinal bioactivity and antifungal defense via downstream products
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Expressed in yeast strain R12 and erg4-knockout complemented strains
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Highly expressed in rhizomes and lateral roots; low in leaves
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Highly expressed in rhizomes and lateral roots
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Channels cycloartenol into the cholesterol branch, supporting precursor formation for timosaponins
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Yeast heterologous expression; erg4 knockout complementation; GC-MS
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. AaSSR2-1 converted cycloartenol to cycloartanol. 2. It complements yeast erg4 essential function in the engineered background. 3. It defines the cholesterol/timosaponin branch competing with SMT1.
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Transcriptome data from a published study [23]; accession NA
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Enhancing SSR2 flux may reduce phytosterol formation because it competes with SMT1 for cycloartenol
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: A small amount of cycloartanol in control was attributed to endogenous yeast erg4 activity; function confirmed more rigorously after erg4 replacement
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Strong target to increase cholesterol-derived timosaponin precursors, especially in combination with AaOSCR12 and downstream CYP genes

Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key branch-switch enzyme; not explicitly established as rate-limiting
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Reduces the Δ24(25) side-chain double bond of cycloartenol to form cycloartanol
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Directs precursor flux toward cholesterol and downstream timosaponins
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Medicinal bioactivity and antifungal defense via downstream products
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Expressed in yeast strain R12 and erg4-knockout complemented strains
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Highly expressed in rhizomes and lateral roots; low in leaves
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Highly expressed in rhizomes and lateral roots
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Channels cycloartenol into the cholesterol branch, supporting precursor formation for timosaponins
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Yeast heterologous expression; erg4 knockout complementation; GC-MS
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. AaSSR2-2 converted cycloartenol to cycloartanol. 2. It complements yeast erg4 essential function in the engineered background. 3. It defines the cholesterol/timosaponin branch competing with SMT1.
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Transcriptome data from a published study [23]; accession NA
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Enhancing SSR2 flux may reduce phytosterol formation because it competes with SMT1 for cycloartenol
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Functionally similar to AaSSR2-1 in assays reported here
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Candidate branch-control enzyme for increasing cholesterol-derived timosaponin flux; may be combined with AaOSCR12 and AaF26G1 depending target chemotype

Pathway_Genes.Biosynthetic_Pathway: Cholesterol downstream modification in timosaponin biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Oxidizes/hydroxylates cholesterol at C-22 to produce 22(R)-hydroxycholesterol
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Forms a direct modified precursor for downstream timosaponin biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Medicinal bioactivity and antifungal defense via downstream products
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Expressed in high-cholesterol yeast strain RH6829 and tested also in high-campesterol strain cam1
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Highly expressed in rhizomes and lateral roots; low in leaves
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Highly expressed in rhizomes and lateral roots
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Produces 22(R)-hydroxycholesterol from cholesterol, establishing a post-cholesterol step toward timosaponins
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Phylogenetic prediction; yeast heterologous expression in RH6829; GC-MS; purification and NMR
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. AaCYP90B27 specifically converted cholesterol to 22(R)-hydroxycholesterol. 2. It showed no detectable activity toward campesterol. 3. It likely functions as a dedicated cholesterol C-22 oxidation enzyme in timosaponin biosynthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Transcriptome data from a published study [23]; accession NA
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Specificity for cholesterol may limit broader sterol perturbation, but no direct tradeoff reported
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Displayed high substrate specificity compared with the broader AaCYP90B2 enzyme
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful for precise reconstruction of cholesterol-derived timosaponin pathway with lower risk of off-target campesterol modification

Pathway_Genes.Biosynthetic_Pathway: Cholesterol downstream modification in timosaponin biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Hydroxylates cholesterol to generate multiple oxidized products, with 16(S),22(S)-dihydroxycholesterol as the major identified product
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Forms direct oxidized intermediates for downstream timosaponin biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Medicinal bioactivity and antifungal defense via downstream products
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Expressed in high-cholesterol yeast strain RH6829 and high-campesterol strain cam1
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Highly expressed in rhizomes and lateral roots; low in leaves
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Highly expressed in rhizomes and lateral roots
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Carries out broad sterol oxidation including formation of 16(S),22(S)-dihydroxycholesterol, contributing to downstream timosaponin precursor diversification
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Phylogenetic prediction; yeast heterologous expression in RH6829 and cam1; GC-MS; comparison with positive control; purification and NMR for major product
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. AaCYP90B2 converted cholesterol into up to eight products, with 16(S),22(S)-dihydroxycholesterol as the major identified metabolite. 2. It also transformed campesterol into seven products, demonstrating broad substrate promiscuity. 3. It may function in both plant sterol and steroidal saponin pathways.
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Transcriptome data from a published study [23]; accession NA
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Broad substrate promiscuity may perturb both phytosterol and steroidal saponin metabolism
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Minor products were difficult to purify; enzyme likely contributes pathway complexity rather than a single clean step
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Powerful but potentially pleiotropic engineering target; best used with metabolite monitoring because it can modify both cholesterol and campesterol
